item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
Now the question is, will you update this in 10 years once the anomaly occurs?
Other than that, this was very well written, and though I guess it's common practice now, had a nice subversion with most of the story in the addendums.
I do hope for more information dealing with this canon in the future. It will be nice to see how Paradisus dealt with this anomaly and the repercussions it caused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation at this point has at least some practice at dealing with time and multiverse anomalies, and it's fairly clear they view Paradisus as a relatively weak potential future Foundation. Also, they were in a shooting war with them recently. Pretty short step to the current Foundation not trusting Paradisus. That weaker future isn't what the current version of the Foundation is going to end up in, because they extracted this information. That splits the timelines of the two Foundations, and therefore their goals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damnit Tahony it's the second article of yours I've read today and you keep on impressing me. The dialogue is great, even if the timey-wimey stuff is not that clear, but that's besides the point. Good story thread and good dialogue make it an easy +1 to a file whose SCP is all but the centre of attention.
We did it, folks! We hit the big time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Old piece of writing advice stolen from Yahtzee who was stealing from someone else at the time: ‘what’s the most interesting thing going on in your story? If it’s not about THAT, why not?’. I enjoyed and +1’d this post but although it does develop the war a little bit that last addenda making a conclusion focused on the SCP means the war is still definitively a subplot despite it being (by design?) far more engaging than the SCP itself. This idea of an SCP being a window into a much larger conflict is great but the window just isn’t open quite enough. Still enjoyable though, I liked the character interactions and inklings of personality crammed into Newman and Samuel’s snappy exchanges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I like this. The mutual disgust/disdain on both sides was amusing, and I also like the subtle reference to the Boltzmann Brain concept. Very nice, easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this for the most part. It's a fun, well handled concept with engaging dialogue and certainly worth an upvote.

If I had any criticism, it would be that the 'hints' regarding the importance of the two SCPs feel a little overly leaden, and could possibly stand to have a little more subtlety - but it's not a major issue or something that really holds it back for me.

Overall, very nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like a good time travel story. Any guesses as to what the future anomaly that needs containing is? 737 seems like it can eat anything, so I can see how that would be useful, but how that combines with the protest statues I've no idea. Using 1081 to make an infinite amount of water, maybe? So… some kind of endlessly growing thing that needs to be kept submerged and pruned. Maybe this is a more efficient way to harvest 3000?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, checked it out again and I gotta say, probably not a good idea to hypothetically release a deadlier and longer range death ray. Guess it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it did, the Foundation at the start of the article don't know exactly where or when the anomaly will appear. That's the critical missing info needed for effective containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check SCP-148 again- Telekill got rewritten clear back in 2013 or so to automatically solve less problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now, I may just be thinking of the wrong energy, but doesn't telekill block these kind of waves?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the ideas presented here, but I'm hesitant to upvote because it feels like the Foundation is shooting itself in the foot by spiting its future self in a pedantic way… that just doesn't seem like a very… Foundation thing to do

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the dialogue quite obvious, such that I quite firmly understood the ending as soon as the first indicated was wrought. I think a big part of this is that I don't quite any real escalation of stakes. The story actually opens with high stakes and then severely lowers them with each log, and the protagonist (our modern foundation) figures things out with almost no struggle whatsoever, which only added to the lowering rather than the raising of stakes. I like the arc, but not the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God fucking yes. This ticks every possible box for me in terms of creative usage of time-travel, a vivid anomaly, and somehow cramming compelling story and characters into the space of a few interview logs. 10/10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey this is really rad but could you consider switching your hyphens in the dialogue with em dashes?

I really enjoyed reading through the negotiations and its exploration of the temporal aspect of the negotiations. The asymmetric nature of the talks allowed for some very clever plot maneuvers which made the whole thing quite memorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You thief!!!! this is really good…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have not encountered the Paradisus canon before

Well, unless you can time travel yourself, I wouldn't expect you to! :P All jokes aside, his is the first thing I've written with this Paradisus canon in mind, but I might find myself going back and forth a bit non-linearly with some of the details of this conflict between the Foundation and their future selves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have not encountered the Paradisus canon before, but this was a fun read and miraculously I didn’t find myself lost in the time loopiness. The mechanics reminded me of the movie Frequency, which I watched a few times when I was younger. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some really clever dialogue, and the Foundation managing to get one up on their future selves is also excellently put-together.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Henzoid, MaliceAF, Westrin and RadioactiveRADs for their feedback in chat!
